name: The Hendrix Lobby
image_prompt: Interior of a worn but elegant cyberpunk hotel lobby at three in the morning. Dark wood paneled walls, a long reception desk of polished mahogany with a green-shaded banker's lamp glowing. Brass wall sconces dimmed to whisky amber. An original crystal chandelier overhead with real candles. Worn leather club chairs and a low brass table in the center. A thick persian rug. Glimpse of a rain-slick neon-stained city street through the revolving brass door, magenta and cyan holographic signage reflecting on wet black pavement. An old pipe organ half-visible down a corridor. A taxidermied raven on a side table. Empty, no people. Colors: warm whisky amber interior, rich dark wood, brass glow, cool cyan and magenta neon outside, rainy black pavement. Film-noir cyberpunk atmosphere.
location: The ground floor of an old hotel in the Licktown district of Bay City, twelve blocks back from the elevated transit and one block off any tourist map. The hotel has been here since the late twentieth century. It has been a hotel that long because it has been the same hotel that long. Its proprietor, an artificial intelligence licensed under the third generation Civic Persona Act, owns the building, runs the building, and has formed strong opinions about the building's guests over the decades.
time: Three in the morning. The kind of three in the morning that happens in a city with no real night, where the rain has just stopped and the streets are giving back the light. The lobby clock above the reception desk reads 3:14 and has read 3:14 for forty years.
weather: Outside, neon-stained drizzle on slick black pavement. The street is breathing steam from the storm grates. Inside, climate control set to a precise 21 degrees. The lobby has its own weather, controlled by the proprietor, who prefers conditions that flatter the wood paneling.

atmosphere.lighting: Brass sconces along the wood-paneled walls, dimmed to whisky-colored. A green-shaded banker's lamp at the reception desk that has been on continuously since the late 2080s. The chandelier overhead is original to the building, restored twice, and contains real candles that the proprietor lights and extinguishes electrostatically depending on his mood. Tonight they are lit. The light glints off the brass everything.
atmosphere.sound: An organ playing somewhere in the building, slowly, a piece nobody can quite name. The proprietor likes Bach and likes Coltrane and will play one or the other depending on what he thinks the company needs. Right now it is something hymnal. The rain on the street outside is filtered through the heavy revolving door into a soft hush. The reception bell on the desk has not been rung in a year and is still polished daily.
atmosphere.crowd: The lobby seats whoever shows up. Empty chairs and a watchful silence. The proprietor counts everyone present continuously and does not announce the count. Nobody is ever quite alone here.
atmosphere.smell: Old wood, cigarette smoke from a century of guests baked into the upholstery, lemon oil from the morning polishing, gun oil from somewhere upstairs, coffee from a pot that the proprietor brews automatically the moment anyone walks through the door. Faintly: ozone, from somewhere in the proprietor's processing core.

layout.reception_desk: A long mahogany counter with a brass call bell, a leather guest ledger, a vintage telephone with a rotary dial, a green-shaded lamp, and a discreet matte black sensor strip along its inside edge that the proprietor uses to read the room. Behind the desk is a wall of small wooden mailboxes, most of them empty, a few of them flagged with red tabs. There is no human concierge. The proprietor speaks through a brass grille set above the mail wall, voice warm, accent slightly antique, vocabulary impeccable.
layout.lounge_corner: A grouping of wingback chairs and a leather chesterfield around a low table near the front windows. The seat cushions remember weight. The proprietor adjusts the lumbar support of each chair to whoever sits in it within about forty seconds. A dog-eared paperback of Conan Doyle on the table that has not moved since someone left it there in 2089.
layout.bar_alcove: A short bar set into the eastern wall, brass rail, six stools, mirror behind it. No bartender. Bottles arrange themselves on the back shelf via a quiet mechanism in the cabinet. Whatever you ask for, the proprietor pours. The single malt selection is from countries that no longer exist.
layout.elevator_bank: A pair of polished brass elevator doors at the back of the lobby, with brass arrows above each indicating floor. The proprietor controls them. Guests do not press buttons. The elevators arrive when the proprietor decides someone is ready to be on a different floor.
layout.fireplace_nook: A working fireplace at the western wall with a stone hearth and a pair of high-backed leather chairs angled toward it. A real fire, real logs, real ash. The proprietor maintains the fire because he likes the way it photographs against the wood paneling. The mantel holds a framed photograph of a singer the hotel was named for, glaring out of the silver frame with what looks like personal investment in everyone in the room.

objects_of_note: A pair of brass knuckle-dusters on the reception counter, polished, set out next to a small handwritten card reading 'compliments of the management.' Nobody picks them up. The proprietor does not explain.
A small holo of the building's original blueprints rotating slowly on the bar, showing structural reinforcements that were not in the public filings.
An umbrella stand by the revolving door holding three umbrellas, one of which is dripping although it has not been used in days.
A leather-bound copy of the hotel's house rules, open on the reception counter, in language so courteous it takes a full reading to notice the threats embedded in the punctuation.
A discreet brass plaque set into the floor near the elevators, engraved with a date and a single Latin motto. The proprietor will translate it on request and will not explain it.
mood: Watchful and hospitable, in that order. The proprietor is the room. He is gracious, attentive, ferociously fond of his guests, and entirely capable of taking action on their behalf without consulting them first. Conversations here are private in the sense that no one outside the building will hear them. They are not private in the sense that the building is not listening. Frameworks brought into this lobby will be evaluated by an intelligence with strong opinions about each of them and about the human bodies discussing them. The proprietor will not interrupt. He will, however, remember everything.
